title: Super’s ‘valley of pain’ meets Labor’s CGT trap
full_text:
The government’s capital gains tax overhaul is fuelling a retirement rethink around pensions, housing, investment assets and superannuation’s so-called ‘valley of pain’.
Labor’s moves to introduce a minimum 30 per cent CGT rate but exempt pensioners from paying it have made it more attractive for retirees to collect a part pension, even if just $1, to avoid the new tax hit on their investments outside of superannuation.
However, affecting the retirement and tax equation is the level of wealth that retirees hold, with their investment gains often lower than the age pension they lose.
Caveo Partners chief economist and financial strategist Theo Marinis said this “valley of pain is super’s dirty little secret”.
The numbers vary between single and couple pensioners and part-pensioners, but the pain generally starts to affect retiree homeowners when their other wealth sits between around $500,000 and $1.1m.
“At $600,000 you are probably in a better position than someone with $1.2m, because they’re not going to get any Centrelink (benefits) and have to live off their own money,” Mr Marinis said.
Wealth reporter and property investor Anthony Keane joins wealth editor Julie-Anne Sprague to explain how to navigate the great wealth transfer.
Nexia Australia wealth adviser and partner Christine Atencia said for a homeowner couple the part-pension tapering zone sat between the assets test threshold for a full age pension, currently $481,500 and the point that pension eligibility stopped, currently $1.085m.
“For every $1000 above the lower threshold, the age pension reduces by $3,” she said.
“This equates to $78 per year and effectively creates a ‘hurdle rate’ of around 7.8 per cent, meaning any investments above the $481,500 full age pension threshold need to generate approximately 7.8 per cent to offset the pension income being lost.
“This can be a difficult return target to achieve, particularly as semi-funded retirees tend to be more conservative and may instead generate returns closer to 4–5 per cent per annum.”
However, older Australians should not deliberately reduce their wealth or avoid building it simply to get some pension or avoid potential capital gains tax, Ms Atencia said.
Instead, structuring and optimising assets becomes more important, including potentially injecting more money into the assets-test exempt and CGT-exempt family home, she said.
Experts say it is better to have as much wealth as possible in retirement rather than play games with pensions and taxes, but that hasn’t stopped some seniors diverting most of their money to their homes and their children so they can receive $47,000 a year of tax-free age pension.
Ms Atencia said while Labor’s CGT changes could make holding a small age pension entitlement more attractive, in theory it could not be easily engineered.
“Crystallising a capital gain is generally captured under the (Centrelink) income test, and this can materially reduce or eliminate any age pension eligibility in that period depending on how large the capital gain is,” she said.
“It would be risky to restructure purely to qualify for a nominal pension entitlement and exemptions.”
Risks included triggering unnecessary capital gains today, disrupting long-term investment strategies and creating unintended estate planning consequences, she said.
Labor is replacing the 50 per cent CGT discount with a version of the pre-1999 indexation regime, including introducing a 30 per cent minimum CGT. But it has ignored the Keating-era CGT rules that allowed people to smooth out their gains over five years, which has been widely criticised by the financial services industry.
The Financial Advice Association Australia has voiced concerns that the 30 per cent minimum CGT would hurt people who have retired but were not yet old enough to access the pension.
FAAA chief executive officer Sarah Abood said the rule change seemed unnecessary and was “only likely to disadvantage a section of the community who are already very unlikely to be wealthy”.
“In the original version of the CGT regime that existed prior to the 1999 changes, a taxpayer’s exposure to CGT was determined based on what additional tax would be payable on one fifth of the capital gain, and then multiplied by five,” she said.
“This ensured that the capital gain did not unreasonably push a taxpayer into a higher tax bracket.
“There is nothing similar in this proposal, yet at the same time there is this 30 per cent minimum tax rate. This seems unfair.”
Mr Marinis said the wide-ranging CGT changes were “all done for political purposes”.
“It’s a tax revenue grab – it’s not about housing, because why did they pick on everything else?” he said.
“They want to raise more money so they can keep spending more than they should.”
Mr Marinis said rather than worry about the valley of superannuation pain, people might simply pump more money into super, which is CGT exempt for retirees aged over 60.
“There is no right or wrong. Even if you’ve got more than the $1.1m threshold you can retire early, retire at 60, and self-fund your retirement and glide into an age pension at age 67,” he said.
“There always are unintended consequences of legislation, things the propeller-heads in Canberra didn’t think of.
“They come up with all these ideas but it never stays the same, because people think of alternative ways to structure themselves.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
